data.title: Rare Earths: State Intervention Needed To Reshape Rare Earths Market
data.description:
Rare earth elements have come under increasing focus owing to their importance to defense equipment manufacturing and clean technology. China has monopolized this supply chain for over two decades and has not shied away from using it for geopolitical leverage, forcing others to think of a diversification strategy. This is Part 4 of a 5-part series in which Platts News explores the strategies under play and the challenges they face.

Government support is emerging as the most effective strategy for nations to cut their dependence on China's rare earths supply chains, as market forces alone could not overcome China's entrenched dominance in the sector, according to industry participants.

From equity investments and subsidies to streamlined permitting and price floors, the US, EU, Australia and other nations are providing unprecedented support to build alternative supply chains for rare earths, a group of 17 elements used in permanent magnets that are critical to defense and clean energy applications.

"Support from Western governments is highly valuable because it levels the playing field with other countries that subsidize their industries," Kim Casey, director of investor relations at Energy Fuels Inc., told Platts, part of S&P Global Energy.

Despite concerns that state participation could disrupt market competition by favoring a few players, these government tools are crucial in establishing supply chains outside of China, whose near-monopoly of the sector has essentially driven competitors away, Casey said.

China's recent restrictions on its rare earths exports have increased the urgency for industry players to seek alternative sources of the material. China controlled 59% of rare earths mine production, 91% of refined production and 94% of magnet manufacturing in 2024, according to the International Energy Agency.

Energy Fuels (UUUU) is seeking US government support to advance the rare earths processing capacity of its White Mesa uranium mill in Utah, Casey said.

'Skin in the game'

With China flooding the market with subsidized rare earths products, new entrants are expected to struggle without government backing, as downstream consumers are unwilling to pay higher prices to support an alternative supply chain.

State support will help mitigate this risk for non-Chinese rare earths companies, according to industry participants.

"Governments have a track record of addressing these gaps using legislative, financial and diplomatic tools to secure raw materials against a backdrop of geopolitical tensions and supply concentration," Hannah Levinger, a senior economist at Germany-based KfW Research, told Platts.

KfW Research is part of KfW Group, a German state-owned investment bank that serves as the central point of contact for Germany's raw materials fund. The fund provides financing for projects in exchange for minority stakes granted to the German federal government.

Government intervention also provides the foundations needed to develop a "functioning market" for rare earths that does not yet exist outside of China, according to Ian Donabie, manager of corporate communications at Australian Strategic Materials Ltd. The company is developing the Dubbo rare earths project in New South Wales, Australia.

"Standing up what is effectively an entirely new industry — one that competes at commercial scale — requires policy alignment and government support to get off the ground," Donabie told Platts.

Risk-averse private investors are also likely to be attracted to rare earths projects that have public funding, said Gracelin Baskaran, director of the Critical Minerals Security Program at the Center for Strategic and International Studies, a think tank based in Washington, D.C.

"Financing from governments is a very powerful catalyst for private capital," Baskaran said in a recent lecture in Manila, Philippines.

"It shows a commitment that the government often has skin in the game, which is really important. It often doesn't have to be a lot of money, but we do often see that to be quite powerful," Baskaran added.

Own merits

Government support is not about picking winners. Instead, it helps set rules to create conditions for sustainable supply chains, a spokesperson for the European Investment Bank (EIB) told Platts. EIB is the lending arm of the EU, which committed €1.2 billion in financing for critical raw material projects in 2025.

"Ultimately, projects must still stand on their own merits: strong economics, solid [environmental, social and governance] performance, and strategic relevance," the EIB spokesperson said.

While state support boosts private investor confidence in rare earths projects, these developments must still demonstrate their ability to deliver a reliable supply, as shifts in policies or governments may result in the reallocation of funds.

"Rare earth companies must still be able to prove they can deliver, and those who can do so at a lower cost will be the ones that survive and thrive," Energy Fuels' Casey said.

Platts on March 31 launched four first-of-their-kind price assessments for rare earth elements, focusing on mine-to-industry alternative supply chains.

data.title: Invesco Global Strategic Income Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key takeaways

1 The fund outperformed for the quarter

The fund (OPSIX) outperformed its benchmark for the quarter. The fund's interest rate positioning, credit and foreign currency exposures all added to relative return.

2 Opportunity to benefit from income and diversification

Global fixed income markets offer in our view a wide opportunity set for additional yield and total return potential, with built-in diversification across geographies and fixed income sectors.

3 We believe weakening US dollar offers greater total return potential for global fixed income

The US dollar declined over 9% in 2025 yet has remained expensive on a historical basis and could be vulnerable to further decline, particularly if US economic growth slows.

Investment objective

The fund seeks total return.

Fund facts

Fund AUM ($M) 1,734.81

Portfolio managers

Hemant Baijal, Arin Kornchankul, Kristina Campmany, Michael Block

Manager perspective and outlook

  • Economic and policy outlooks have continued to diverge across regions and countries, offering a robust opportunity set beyond the US that may encourage investors to consider diversifying.
  • Despite lingering economic and inflation uncertainties, global growth has remained positive, with generally easier global monetary policy and supportive fiscal spending.
  • The US government shutdown (which paused data releases) ultimately ended mid-quarter, followed by the release of stabilizing labor market data and lower-than-expected US CPI.
  • The US Federal Reserve (Fed) cut the federal funds rate by 0.50% and signaled more data-dependence going forward. The Bank of England and Reserve Bank of New Zealand also cut interest rates, while the Reserve Bank of Australia, Bank of Canada and European Central Bank held steady and the Bank of Japan hiked rates.
  • Emerging market central banks largely continued their easing cycles, though more conservatively amid better-than-expected growth. We believe improving inflation leaves the door open for further cuts in 2026.
  • Though higher for the quarter, the US dollar weakened in 2025, down over 9%. We believe downward pressure is likely to continue.
  • As economic outcomes and policy decisions further diverge, we believe this is an exceptional environment for active management to generate alpha (excess return above a given benchmark).

Portfolio positioning

During the quarter, foreign currency exposure decreased, mainly by reducing developed market currencies, and to a lesser extent, emerging market currencies. Duration exposure increased, primarily by adding developed market duration. Credit exposure increased, primarily from an increase in developed market corporates.

Our global outlook remains positive to benign, upheld by generally easier monetary policy and fiscal support, albeit with divergence across countries and tempered by growing global imbalances. In the US, we expect growth to benefit from supportive tax policy, loose fiscal conditions and accommodative monetary policy. Fiscal spending has been supporting Eurozone growth, given greater focus on security and increasing capital expenditures. Emerging markets (excluding China) have also been seeing momentum, with solid growth in India and South Africa, while Eastern European economies have been bolstered by Europe's upswing. We expect inflation to remain under control as we believe the US dollar should be stable or decline.

Nevertheless, growing global imbalances in our view pose risks. China has maintained an excessive trade surplus while exporting deflation. In the US, we believe sticky inflation and labor market uncertainty demand a balancing act, while Fed leadership and member changes could generate more aggressive easing and likely greater dissent. In the Eurozone, we see a supply/demand mismatch in longer maturity bond markets. We see the greatest imbalance in Japan. The yen has depreciated despite a large current account surplus and high savings rate, as lagging economic growth has depressed investment returns, causing a large buildup of unhedged offshore assets.

In 2025, divergence was, in our view, clearly visible in foreign currency markets, with the US dollar's decline marking what we see as a significant shift. Currencies such as the euro and Swiss franc appreciated about 15% versus the dollar, while the Japanese yen diverged mid-year and was unchanged at year end. Given Japan's large buildup of unhedged offshore assets, this could lead to disorderly appreciation of the yen, as seen in Taiwan earlier and South Korea to a lesser extent at year end.

Greater dispersion and potential volatility, in our view, reinforce the value of diversification and importance of an active approach in seeking excess return while managing risk. Given the wide range of economic and policy outcomes globally, we believe today's environment rewards active country selection across interest rate, credit and foreign currency exposures.

Performance highlights

The fund's Class A shares at net asset value (NAV) outperformed its benchmark for the quarter. The fund's interest rate positioning, credit and foreign currency exposures all added to relative return. The top contributors to relative return were interest rate positioning in the US and Brazil, along with positioning in the South African rand. The largest detractors from relative return were interest rate positioning in Australia, along with positioning in the Chinese renminbi and euro.

data.title: Emerging Market Manufacturing Input Prices Rise Steeply Amid Middle East War
data.description:
Emerging market economies recorded a sharp upswing in manufacturing costs during March, as the outbreak of war in the Middle East led to widespread increases in the price of fuel, transport and other commodities, as well as a rise in US dollar-denominated import prices. The latest PMI data showed that overall emerging market manufacturing input prices rose at the fastest rate in close to four years, with marked accelerations observed in parts of Latin and Central America, Southeast Asia and Central Europe.

Emerging market manufacturing cost pressures accelerate in March

The seasonally adjusted Emerging Markets Manufacturing PMI Input Prices Index rose sharply in March, climbing to its highest since June 2022 and signalling a marked increase in input costs across global emerging economies. Although the rate of inflation was less sharp than observed over developed markets, the uptick in price pressures compared to February was similarly steep.

Of the 16 emerging market economies where manufacturing sector PMI data are available, 14 reported an acceleration of input price inflation in March. Some of the fastest overall rises in input costs were seen in Latin and Central American economies, such as Brazil and Mexico, but marked upswings in Input Prices Index readings were also recorded in countries across Central Europe and Southeast Asia, including Vietnam, Czech Republic, Poland and the Philippines.

Comments analysis highlight impact of war on supply side

Analysing the qualitative data provided by panellists to our global PMI surveys helps us to understand the extent to which the Middle East conflict shaped economic conditions in March. Comments showed that manufacturers faced increases in costs for a number of items, primarily oil, as well as oil-intensive inputs such as fuel, plastics and rubber. Shipping bottlenecks have also resulted in some manufacturers facing higher freight prices, as well as delays in input deliveries.

Manufacturers in most of the emerging markets covered commented that the Middle East war had a direct impact on both overall input costs and supplier delivery times. Producers in Malaysia were the most likely to link both cost rises and vendor delays to the conflict. Here, PMI data signalled the greatest deterioration in supplier delivery times since May 2022, whilst cost pressures also accelerated and business sentiment weakened.

As well as the direct impact of the war, survey anecdotal reports signalled that emerging market manufacturers were also impacted by a strengthening of the US dollar. Since the Middle East war began, the dollar has risen compared to a basket of emerging market currencies as increased investor risk in global financial markets sparked a flight to safety. This led producers to see prices on imported items – often denominated in US dollars – rise since February.

Rise in manufacturing costs feeds through into output charges

The seasonally adjusted Emerging Markets Manufacturing PMI Output Prices Index also increased in March, showing that producers were more aggressive with their price setting as they attempted to pass through higher input prices to customers. The uplift in overall factory gate prices was the most marked in almost four years, although it remained less pronounced than the equivalent rise seen in developed markets.

These developments present fresh upside inflation risk in emerging markets, as increases in manufacturing goods prices are likely to feed through into downstream markets and consumer prices. In Brazil, for instance, where manufacturers hiked their charges at a much faster pace than in February, PMI data showed cost pressures across the services economy also accelerating sharply. While consumers are already feeling the effects of the Middle East war on their transport costs, the data suggests it may not be too long before prices on other key consumer items also start to increase.

data.title: COPX: Copper Rush Is Just Getting Started
data.description:
Investment Thesis

With most global investments committed to AI infrastructure, coupled with rising demand for electricity and data centers, investors are turning their focus to the key materials needed in their construction. While investors closely follow AI development in the market, most companies are increasing their investments in AI. The MAG 7 companies are leading the race with a combined capex of around $400 billion in 2025 and expected capex of $700 billion in 2026. As more companies realize AI investments are crucial to remain competitive in the future. As Mark Zuckerberg noted, “it is better to overbuild now than to regret later,” which best explains the current market sentiment.

The supply side is under continued pressure: new copper discoveries are rare, mine developments are expensive and slow to develop, and copper ore quality continues to decline. The current supply imbalance, coupled with massive AI capex, is contributing to higher copper prices.

At the same time, investors are worried about rising uncertainty regarding U.S. tariff implementation on copper imports, coupled with currency debasement fears and a decreasing chance of interest rate cuts by the Federal Reserve. This has placed commodities as a key addition to the investors' portfolios.

With commodities back in focus, investors can gain exposure to the copper theme by investing in the Global X Copper Miners ETF (COPX). COPX holds 48 mining companies, providing diversification benefits but also leverage to rising copper prices. With growing U.S. policy support for critical minerals and a weakening U.S. dollar, momentum is increasing for copper mining companies, which are also benefiting from higher realized pricing as well as expanding margins due to tightening copper supply in the industry.

In today's market, copper fundamentals are less driven by short-term cyclical movements and more by structural imbalances. Limited supply, increasing AI investment, and supportive government policy support the thesis that the copper sector is at the start of a bullish phase. For investors looking for a diversified but leveraged exposure to the copper sector, COPX could be an interesting choice for their portfolio.

About the ETF

There are several ways investors can add this strategic commodity to their portfolio: by investing directly in a copper mining company, purchasing physical copper, trading copper futures, or investing in an ETF.

For most investors, owning a specific mining company brings above-average volatility to their portfolios, while holding physical copper involves additional storage expenses. To reduce company-specific risk and storage issues, investors can gain exposure to the copper theme by investing in the Global X Copper Miners ETF, which follows the underlying Solactive Global Copper Miners Total Return Index. By owning COPX instead of a single mining company, investors reduce company-specific risk through diversified holdings of 48 miners. Compared to holding physical copper, an additional benefit comes through operating leverage. During periods of rising copper prices, mining companies experience increased profitability while most of their costs remain fixed, which translates into expanding margins, resulting in higher earnings growth for investors relative to holding physical copper.

The ETF currently holds 48 companies, with the top ten companies accounting for close to 50% of the holdings. For investors, this represents a semi-concentrated portfolio in larger mining companies such as Freeport-McMoRan Inc., Hudbay Minerals Inc., and Lundin Mining Corporation. By investing in more established companies, COPX provides more stability to investors relative to owning a specific mining stock or a junior mining ETF, especially during market downturns.

Looking at company locations, the majority are in Canada (37.94%), followed by China (10.09%), the U.S. (9.35%), and Australia (8.11%), with the remaining companies diversified across other locations in the world. Diversified holdings across multiple countries in different regions can benefit investors by providing exposure to the emerging, frontier, and developed markets; currency diversification; and potentially stronger returns in individual countries with high GDP growth. However, this also brings additional regulatory and country-specific risk.

COPX charges investors an expense ratio of 0.65%, which is slightly above the average of 0.50% for the thematic ETF sector. This is followed by a beta of 1.40, which reflects high leverage to copper price movements. The ETF currently has $6.84 billion of assets under management, making it one of the largest copper ETFs in the sector and providing investors with increased liquidity and small bid-ask spreads.

With the current combination of high beta and operating leverage through diversified holdings, COPX presents an attractive opportunity for high-conviction investors in the copper sector and AI theme.

Copper Demand Outlook

The buildout of AI infrastructure is increasing the copper intensity of data center expansion. Based on an IEA report, global copper demand in 2024 was 27 million tonnes. For example, a conventional data center uses between 10,000 and 15,000 tons of copper. On the other hand, an AI data center can require 50,000 tons of copper per facility, which represents around three times as much copper as is used in the construction of a conventional data center. Current estimates point to around 8,800 data centers worldwide, and this number is expected to increase to around 12,000 by 2035. As shown in the table below, the North American region leads in the number of data centers under construction, followed by Europe.

Looking at the current copper intensity of data center construction, the addition of nearly 4,000 new data centers by 2035 supports continued demand for copper. If the new AI data centers require around 50,000 tons of copper, the construction of nearly 4,000 additional facilities would translate into tens of millions of tons of copper demand over time.

Looking in the future, projected AI capital investment in data center infrastructure could range between $3 trillion and $8 trillion by 2030, based on the three different scenarios shown in the chart below. Moreover, copper demand is not limited to data centers but includes the power grid infrastructure. From the total investment in AI, around 25% would be deployed for power generation, transmission, cooling, and electrical equipment.

As investment in the global AI infrastructure increases, the amount of copper required to construct these facilities points to continued growth in copper demand over the coming decade.

Another driver of demand for copper is the electric vehicle (EV) industry. Electric vehicles use more copper than conventional internal combustion engine (ICE) vehicles due to their battery systems and electric motors. For example, an ICE vehicle contains on average around 20 kilograms of copper, while an electric vehicle contains on average around 60 kilograms of copper. This means the copper intensity for EVs is around three times higher than that of the average ICE vehicle.

Another important factor that influences the copper intensity in electric vehicles is consumer preferences for the size of the vehicle and battery capacity. Preferences for vehicle size vary across the globe, but in the U.S. many consumers prefer SUVs. These vehicles are heavier and require stronger batteries to enable longer travel, which increases the amount of copper used in vehicle production. As shown in the chart below, global EV production is projected to increase from 50 million to 250 million, which represents a 400% increase by 2030. This figure also includes two- and three-wheeler vehicles as well as buses and trucks. As EV production increases globally, the higher copper intensity used in the development of these vehicles will be an important driver of future copper demand.

A further contributor to copper demand is renewable energy. By 2040, global electricity consumption is expected to increase by 47%. To meet this accelerating demand growth, investments in renewable energy will be needed in areas such as wind turbines, solar panels, and batteries.

Copper is a key material in the green transition due to its durability, conductivity, and cost efficiency. Its importance is highlighted in the chart below, where demand from the green transition alone is expected to increase by around 7.1 million metric tonnes by 2040.

Copper demand is driven by multiple industries, including AI, EV, and renewable energy, all of which are occurring simultaneously and contributing to increased demand. Taken together, all the different drivers of demand across various industries mean companies will need to secure copper to fulfill their long-term strategy. This will gradually make copper less cyclical and a more structurally important metal.

Copper Supply Constraints

With rising demand, the question is whether the supply side has the ability to produce enough copper. One of the main issues miners face is declining ore quality, with average ore declining from 1.9% in 1990 to around 0.9% in the last few years. The decrease in ore quality pushes miners to extract more ores for the same amount of copper, which increases their overall cost of production.

Another issue is a long permit and development process. The usual time from the start of mining construction to first ore extraction can span anywhere from 10 to 17 years.

In addition, current geopolitical conflicts, rising energy prices, and increasing labor costs are contributing to the rising costs of new mine projects. Large initial capital investments are another issue. For example, the typical cost of a small- to medium-sized copper mine can require around $500 million as an initial investment, while large-scale projects can require from $1 billion to $15 billion. Large initial investments that will not produce profit for many years are further slowing down how supply can quickly adapt to the demand.

Lastly, copper reserves are concentrated in a few locations around the world. Over half of all copper findings are located in only five countries: Chile, Australia, Peru, the Democratic Republic of Congo, and Russia. This level of concentration increases country-specific risks that can slow supply down.

The mentioned limitations make copper supply inelastic in the short and medium term. As demand continues to rise across different industries, the capabilities of miners to quickly adapt are limited, which puts pressure on copper prices.

My Outlook on Copper Price

I expect copper prices to remain elevated over the long term due to rising demand and tightening supply. AI infrastructure, EVs, and renewable energy will further increase the copper usage across industries. In addition, declining copper ore quality, together with the long mine development timeline process, will limit how quickly the supply side can adjust to rising demand.

In the short-term period of 1 to 2 years, copper prices will be largely impacted by volatility due to interest rate uncertainty and geopolitical tensions. Since COPX has a beta of 1.40, investors may experience larger downside movements than the broad market during periods of extreme volatility. However, this may present an attractive entry point for long-term investors.

In the medium-term period of 3 to 5 years, the copper market is expected to tighten due to increased data center expansion and supporting AI infrastructure. While large mining companies may gradually increase production, supply is still expected to fall behind rising demand. In an environment like this, COPX is likely to benefit from rising prices and improving operating margins.

Over the long term of 10 years and beyond, the copper market is likely to enter a structural deficit. This is supported by the declining quality of copper ore, limited new findings of copper, and increased demand from AI infrastructure construction. In such an environment, COPX could outperform the broader market, supported by a continued bull market in copper.

COPX Among Its Peers

Why choose COPX over other ETFs in the copper space? There are several ETFs competing within the copper theme; for this peer comparison, I selected ICOP, COPP, and COPJ.

Looking at their portfolio concentration, COPX has a top holding concentration of 52.82% across 48 companies. ICOP has a higher concentration of 61.12% with 54 holdings, while COPP is the most concentrated with 75.45% across 70 companies. COPJ has the lowest concentration of 46.48% with 56 holdings. COPX and COPJ are the least concentrated portfolios, while COPP has the highest portfolio concentration.

The expense ratio is highest for COPJ at 0.76% and lowest for ICOP at 0.47%. COPX and COPP both have an expense ratio of 0.65%. Assets under management (AUM) is highest for COPX at $6.84 billion, while ICOP, COPP, and COPJ each have AUM below $1 billion. Average daily trading value is highest for COPX at $447.40 million, pointing to strong liquidity in the copper space. In comparison, ICOP, COPP, and COPJ have average daily trading values between $20 million and $10 million. Dividend yield is highest for COPJ, while COPX, COPP, and ICOP offer dividend yields ranging between 1.84% and 2.50%. Their year-to-date price performance shows COPX at 7.06%, ICOP at 9.84%, COPP at 3.99%, and COPJ at 0.48%.

COPX has one of the least concentrated portfolios, which makes it a more desirable choice for investors seeking diversification and lower volatility in the copper space. While COPX has a higher expense ratio relative to ICOP, its superior liquidity and larger AUM help investors to overlook cost. COPX offers a moderate dividend yield, while COPJ provides a higher dividend yield but also greater volatility due to its focus on junior miners. For their year-to-date performance, all four ETFs have outperformed the S&P 500, with COPX and ICOP showing the strongest price appreciation. Finally, comparing all four ETFs, COPX is a well-diversified, relatively less concentrated, and highly liquid ETF with an attractive dividend yield, making it the most balanced and desirable option among the four.

Institutional Ownership

Compared to more established commodity ETFs such as silver (SLV) and gold (GLD), COPX shows a large discrepancy in institutional ownership. As shown in the chart below, institutional ownership is approximately $11.70 billion for SLV, $53.70 billion for GLD, and $1.20 billion for COPX. A large discrepancy like this suggests that copper is still in the early stages of institutional adoption, with potential for future capital inflows as its importance increases.

Risks

Following strong momentum and coming back into focus for many investors, there are several short- and long-term risks that cannot be disregarded and could impact COPX.

One of the main risks is a slowdown in capital investments in AI. Most companies have so far financed their capital expenditures using free cash flow; however, once this is depleted, they may turn to debt financing, which could become costly if AI investments do not generate sufficient returns to cover the cost of the initial investment. Additionally, there are rising concerns regarding circular financing deals and their likelihood of completion.

Another key risk is slower economic activity in the U.S., which could decrease spending on AI, construction activity, and infrastructure, leading to lower demand for copper.

Finally, there is a risk of replacement, where copper could be replaced by alternative metals in some part of the process. One such metal is aluminum, which is cheaper and could partially replace copper in some cases.

Conclusion

Rising demand across industries is positioning copper as a structural commodity rather than a purely cyclical one. COPX benefits from the “copper rush” through a diversified portfolio of mature mining companies with high leverage to copper price appreciation. Demand is expected to rise in the future, while supply is likely to lag, creating a structural imbalance in the copper market and benefiting mining companies. In addition, relatively low institutional ownership compared to more established commodity ETFs suggests that copper is still at an early stage of institutional adoption.

COPX provides investors with a balanced combination of diversification, relative stability through more established companies, and a solid dividend. I rate COPX as a buy.

data.title: Oral GLP-1 Is Here - Have Credit Markets Caught Up?
data.description:
By Kendall Marthaler & Sabina Shaqiri

The FDA's approval of oral semaglutide removes the injection barrier and may reprice credit exposures built on an older adoption curve.

A New Inflection Point

For the past two years, GLP-1 medications have been a fixture of consumer trend analysis, discussed by everyone from CNBC to sell-side strategists to management consultants. Most of that coverage has centered on the same behavioral observations: People eat less, lose weight, buy new clothes, drink less alcohol.

That framing, while directionally accurate, misses the more consequential question for credit investors: Which issuers are structurally positioned for a world with significantly more GLP-1 users, and which are carrying debt premised on volume assumptions that may no longer hold?

That question has become more urgent. In December 2025, the FDA approved the first oral semaglutide pill for chronic weight management, with a full U.S. launch underway in early 2026 at a self-pay price of $149 per month. The injection barrier, one of the most cited constraints on GLP-1 adoption, has been removed. With current U.S. penetration at approximately 5% of adults and consensus estimates pointing toward 10%,1 the adoption curve is likely steeper from here than the existing literature assumes. Consumer credit exposures priced on the old curve deserve a second look.

GPL-1 Use Is Expected to Rise Further

Off-Label GLP-1 Use for Weight Loss, U.S. Patients in Millions

Reformulation as a Credit Differentiator

The food sector response to GLP-1 adoption is well documented in broad strokes: Users reduce caloric intake by an estimated 16 – 39%, and companies are responding with higher-protein, smaller-portion and more nutrient-dense products. What is less discussed is the credit dimension of that response. Reformulation at scale is capital-intensive. It requires R&D investment, supply chain reconfiguration and extended timelines before incremental revenue materializes. Food issuers with strong balance sheets can better absorb that cost structure. Overleveraged credits, however, particularly those in the snack and processed food categories most exposed to GLP-1-driven volume pressure, are expected to face a more difficult path.

The result is a credit differentiator hiding inside what looks like a uniform industry trend. Two issuers can face the same behavioral headwind, but only one has the financial flexibility to respond to it in a way that protects long-term cash flows. In this environment, credit investors should focus on credits with meaningful protein and less-processed food category exposure; and we scrutinize closely those whose volume projections assume stable consumption behavior in categories that GLP-1 adoption is quietly eroding.

Apparel and Beauty: Which Credits Capture the Upside?

Real apparel expenditure outperformed total consumer spending by approximately 400 basis points per month on average in 2025, a trend our analysis attributes in part to GLP-1-driven wardrobe replacement.2 The credit selection question, however, is more granular than the trend itself. The 400bps outperformance figure is an aggregate; it does not distribute evenly across the issuer landscape. Strong brands with pricing power and loyal customer bases are more likely to capture GLP-1-driven wardrobe replacement spending. In beauty, the GLP-1-adjacent opportunity (skin care, nutritional supplementation, body contouring) concentrates among a number of issuers with the brand equity and distribution scale to address it credibly.

The Behavioral Wild Card, a Long-Horizon Risk Worth Monitoring

Perhaps the most structurally novel risk in the GLP-1 story is the one furthest from consensus: Emerging research from Stanford suggests that, compared to non-users, GLP-1 users show a 40% reduction in opioid cravings, as well as 40 – 50% reductions in opioid overdose and alcohol intoxication rates. Researchers are now investigating whether the same neurological mechanism, a dampening of dopamine-driven reward behavior, may also reduce compulsive and impulse purchasing.

This is early-stage research and should be treated as such. There is no spending data yet that confirms a GLP-1 effect on retail purchase behavior at scale. We are not drawing a conclusion here; we are flagging an open question that credit investors in consumer-facing categories should be monitoring. If the behavioral effect proves durable and generalizable, it has implications for categories built on habitual or emotional purchasing. Some of the debt carried by issuers in those categories is underwritten against volume assumptions that implicitly assume stable consumption psychology. That assumption is worth examining.

Diageo has already acknowledged modest moderation in drinking behavior as a GLP-1 headwind.3 That acknowledgment, by one of the largest and most analytically resourced players in the spirits industry, is a signal worth taking seriously—even if the magnitude of the effect remains unclear.

A Framework, Not a One-Time Call

The most important thing we can say about GLP-1s and consumer credit is not a specific trade recommendation. It is a methodological point: GLP-1 adoption should be treated as an ongoing stress test applied to consumer-facing credit exposures, not as a discrete event to be assessed once and filed away. Opportunities are available to identify issuers that are favorably positioned given their product portfolio, positioning in the market and balance sheet capacity/flexibility to reposition if needed.

The oral pill launch likely means the population of GLP-1 users will grow materially over the next several years. Adoption rates, real-world efficacy data and issuer-level responses are all evolving. A credit framework that evaluates issuers on their reformulation capacity, brand resilience, volume dependency and behavioral exposure to dopamine-driven purchasing (and updates that evaluation as adoption data improves) is, we believe, more durable than any point-in-time sector call.

Footnotes

1 Goldman Sachs as of December 8, 2025.

2 Source: Bloomberg based on Bureau of Economic Analysis data.

3 Source: Diageo Q1 2026 earnings call on February 25, 2026.

This material is provided for informational and educational purposes only and nothing herein constitutes investment, legal, accounting or tax advice. This material is general in nature and is not directed to any category of investors and should not be regarded as individualized, a recommendation, investment advice or a suggestion to engage in or refrain from any investment-related course of action. Investment decisions and the appropriateness of this material should be made based on an investor's individual objectives and circumstances and in consultation with his or her advisors. Information is obtained from sources deemed reliable, but there is no representation or warranty as to its accuracy, completeness or reliability. All information is current as of the date of this material and is subject to change without notice. Any views or opinions expressed may not reflect those of the firm as a whole. Neuberger Berman products and services may not be available in all jurisdictions or to all client types.

This material may include estimates, outlooks, projections and other “forward-looking statements.” Due to a variety of factors, actual events or market behavior may differ significantly from any views expressed. Investing entails risks, including possible loss of principal. Indexes are unmanaged and are not available for direct investment. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

data.title: Cal-Maine Foods May Be Undervalued Because Its Recent Acquisition Isn't Priced In
data.description:
Cal-Maine Foods (CALM) sells eggs, and right now, eggs are getting cheaper. That's good for consumers but might not be good for Cal-Maine. So, Cal-Maine is going to sell more specialty eggs and make more processed food. That will increase the company's margins and stabilize its revenue. But because egg prices are down, Cal-Maine's recent results are still weak, and analysts have low expectations for the company.

Cal-Maine reported net sales of $667 million in fiscal Q3 2026. Its total sales were down 53.0% because egg prices went down. But, while conventional egg sales were down 72.1%, volume was only down 6.7%. So consumers are still buying almost as many eggs; they're just paying less for them. Cal-Maine's specialty egg sales were down 12.1%, but volume was up 5.8%. And Cal-Maine's gross profit margin fell from 50.5% to 17.8% for the quarter, which is a huge swing.

While Cal-Maine is a cyclical company, it's not clear if it's reached the bottom of the egg price cycle yet. Egg prices might still fall further. But Cal-Maine's management is good at long-term planning, so this situation has also created opportunities for the company. Cal-Maine is now buying back stock, and it's also buying other egg companies. And its latest acquisition might not be priced in.

Cal-Maine Made an Acquisition Recently

Cal-Maine just spent $128.5 million to buy Creighton Brothers. While Cal-Maine didn't provide detailed financial information for Creighton Brothers, Cal-Maine has a TTM EV/sales ratio of 0.73. So, if it bought Creighton Brothers at a similar valuation, I'd estimate that the deal added $176 million in annual revenue. It also sounds like the company's fiscal Q3 2026 results do not include the additional revenue from Creighton Brothers. In its report, Cal-Maine said, "Subsequent to third quarter, acquired certain assets of Creighton Brothers LLC and its affiliates, increasing integration across the value chain."

Additionally, Cal-Maine might make more acquisitions in the future if more opportunities are available. This company has a unique dividend policy. While it might not be a good stock for income investors because it cuts its dividend when its income drops, that also frees up cash for other things such as acquisitions. So, the Creighton Brothers acquisition is not the only reason to consider investing in this company. If you don't need consistent dividend income, this could be a good dividend stock.

Cal-Maine's Dividends Vary a Lot

Cal-Maine pays around a third of its net income in dividends. So, this quarter it earned $1.06 and paid $0.360 in dividends. A year ago, for Q3 2025, it paid a dividend of $3.495. That's nearly 10 times higher than its dividend this quarter. I wouldn't call this a dividend cut, though, because Cal-Maine's dividend policy didn't change. When egg prices go back up again, the company will pay higher dividends again.

This company also has a history of paying high dividends. According to SA, its 4-year average dividend yield is 5.76%. Its forward yield is likely to be lower if egg prices remain low. But if there's another bird flu outbreak and egg prices rise again, Cal-Maine's dividend yield could rise sharply. While farms might have more preventative measures in place now, bird flu is still an ongoing concern, and outbreaks still happen regularly. For the time being, though, it looks like Cal-Maine's dividends will probably be lower in the short term.

Cal-Maine's Strong Balance Sheet Supports Buybacks

Recently, I've been examining other large food companies. Several of them have ratios of 3-4X net debt to adjusted EBITDA. So, if the economy gets worse and they can't take out more loans, they might have to cut their dividends. But Cal-Maine has a different philosophy. It only pays out dividends that it can easily afford to pay. And because of that decision, Cal-Maine has no long-term debt, unlike the other large food companies. It also has $1.152 billion in cash right now.

So, this company has a lot of cash available, but its current EPS wouldn't support higher dividend payouts. And it's not clear when egg prices will go back up. So, Cal-Maine has decided to reward investors by buying back stock instead, which seems like a better idea. Its diluted share count fell from 49.0 million shares a year ago to 47.4 million shares in Q3 2026.

Cal-Maine's Specialty Eggs Help Stabilize Its Revenue

I'm also following Vital Farms (VITL). And while Cal-Maine's commodity egg prices vary widely based on the current price of eggs, Vital Farms has been selling eggs for consistently high prices. High-income consumers might be willing to pay high prices for pasture-raised eggs even when low-cost eggs are available. The situation might be changing because Vital Farms isn't going to raise its prices in 2026, so it might have less pricing power now. But I still think Vital Farms' stock has sold off too much.

Cal-Maine has observed the strong demand for pasture-raised eggs, and it has started selling them as well. This should help explain why Cal-Maine's net sales from specialty eggs were much more stable than its commodity egg sales in Q3 2026. On the call, CFO Max Bowman said, "Yes. As Sherman said, Heather, our specialty pricing doesn't fluctuate that much. We call out the vast majority of our specialty pricing as either grain-based or fixed-price-type arrangements with cost plus. So again, it stays pretty flat. There is a component of that, as you call out, that ties to the cage-free California market."

Prepared Food Sales Are Also Stabilizing Cal-Maine's Results

Cal-Maine's prepared food sales made up 9.5% of its revenue in Q3 2025, up from 0.8% last year. And it looks like prepared food sales also helped the company stabilize its revenue, but that's not the only benefit of expanding this business. Prepared food should have higher margins than commodity eggs. Of course, Cal-Maine's earnings are really low right now for cyclical reasons, so this segment might also look bigger than it really is right now as well.

The Main Risk Is That Specialty Egg Competition Could Increase

Cal-Maine is using specialty egg sales to defend its margins and generate more consistent revenue. Its commodity egg business isn't performing as well. Right now, it looks like Cal-Maine's overall strategy is working. Pasture-raised eggs are still popular. But now, a lot of startups have seen the strong demand for pasture-raised eggs, and other companies may attempt to enter the market. If that happens, both Cal-Maine and Vital Farms will face additional margin pressures. And that's a risk factor for both egg companies.

Comparative Valuation for Cal-Maine

Cal-Maine appears to be near the bottom of an egg price cycle, so I'd expect it to have a high P/E. But it doesn't. Right now, Cal-Maine has a forward P/E of 9.50. Vital Farms, its closest competitor, has a forward P/E of 12.47. And the median forward P/E for consumer staples companies is 16.12. So, both of these companies are trading at a discount to other food companies. This only makes sense if analysts expect egg prices to fall even further in 2026, even though egg prices are considered low right now.

Cal-Maine Valuation

Cal-Maine also reported $352.6 million in net income for the first 3 quarters of 2026. Its net income for Q3 2026 was $50.5 million, and its revenue was $667 million. So, conservatively, if its Q4 results are also weak, it could report similar income in Q4 as well. And I'd estimate that Cal-Maine could post full-year net income of $403.1 million. This estimate doesn't include the added income from Creighton Brothers.

It looks like analysts have similar expectations for 2027, which is why Wall Street analysts say Cal-Maine has a 2027 P/E of 18.63. If I assume that Cal-Maine's full-year results for 2027 will look like its Q3 2026 results, the company would report $2.668 billion in revenue and $202 million in net profit. And Cal-Maine's net margin would be 7.6%. But because I estimated that Creighton Brothers generates $176 million in annual revenue, I'd add its revenue to my revenue estimate for Cal-Maine. So, Cal-Maine would report full-year 2027 revenue of $2.844 billion.

So, if Cal-Maine reported $2.844 billion in 2027 revenue and maintained a 7.6% net margin, it would report net income of $216 million. And if I multiply a net profit of $216 million by a 2027 P/E of 18.63, I get an estimated market cap of $4.024 billion. Again, Cal-Maine has 47.4 million diluted shares, so my one-year target price would be $84.90. That's 10% higher than Cal-Maine's current price of $77.07.

Conclusion

It looks like analysts' expectations for Cal-Maine are fairly low. Egg prices are likely to remain low for the next year. Cal-Maine has been able to stabilize its revenue by adding prepared food and selling pasture-raised eggs, and that limits the downside risks to some extent. But even if you assume that Cal-Maine's fiscal 2027 results will be similar to its recent results, the Creighton Brothers acquisition does not appear to be priced in because of the deal timing. And as a result, it looks like Cal-Maine could still be undervalued. So, I'd say Cal-Maine is a buy.

data.title: Ubtech Robotics: Strong Beat And Positive Outlook
data.description:
I'm initiating coverage on Ubtech Robotics Corp Ltd (UBTRF) (9880.HK) with a "Buy" rating.

Its FY2025 net loss wasn't as bad as what the market feared. Looking ahead, the company's profitability should improve in '26.

The stock is also undervalued. It currently trades at a discount to its domestic peer based on the forward price-to-sales ratio.

Corporate Overview

UBTRF is described by Neuberger Berman as the developer of "Walker S2." The latter is "the first humanoid that can change its own batteries without human intervention."

Competencies

Solutions

Humanoid robots accounted for 44% of the firm's FY2025 top line. The balance was attributable to non-humanoid products (31%) and other devices (25%).

Also, it derived 26%, 21%, and 14% of last year's sales from the consumer, education, and logistics verticals, respectively. The other 39% was generated by a diverse range of industries.

UBTRF's home market of Mainland China made up over three-quarters of its latest fiscal year turnover.

Losses Shrank at a Faster-than-Expected Pace

Last Tuesday, the group released an announcement unveiling its most recent financials.

UBTRF registered a FY25 bottom line of -CNY703.2 million. That's better than the preceding year's CNY1.1 billion loss. The sell-side had also projected a wider deficit of CNY1,020 million (source: Bloomberg).

My take is that product portfolio shifts and leaner operations strengthened the company's performance.

The group-level 38% "Gross Profit Margin" or "GPM" for '25 represented a 900bps improvement. That's 6.4ppts above the consensus estimate too.

I'm impressed with how it has pivoted towards more profitable segments.

The humanoid-related solutions' total sales contribution went up from FY2024's 5.9% to FY2025's 43.5%. At the results briefing, UBTRF indicated that its "full-sized embodied smart humanoids" boasted a superior GPM of around 55%. I obtained the transcript from S&P Capital IQ.

Also, its "Selling, General & Administrative" or (SG&A)-to-revenue metric fell substantially by 27.4ppts to 2025's 40.3%

I believe UBTRF benefited from operating leverage. The enterprise's topline jumped 53% to CNY2.0 billion in the prior year. That provided a larger base to spread non-variable expenses.

It enhanced efficiency in other business areas as well. The new "Walker S3" humanoid model is almost one-tenth lighter than its predecessor thanks to the utilization of innovative materials. The firm also employed the latest binocular vision technology to cut sensor-related costs by roughly 70%. These figures came from management's comments at the FY25 meeting.

UBTRF's prospects are equally bright, as I will outline in the next section.

Anticipating Growth Acceleration and Profitability Improvement

As per S&P Capital IQ, the analysts are forecasting that UBTRF will record FY2026 revenues and a bottom line of CNY4.02 billion and -CNY114 million, respectively.

This implies a more rapid rate of topline expansion (+101%) and significantly narrower losses. I'm optimistic that the company can meet or even surpass the market's expectations.

UBTRF's aim is to ship 10k humanoids in '26, according to the earnings call disclosures. That's way above 2025's actual sales volume of 1,079.

About half of these deliveries relate to the industrial-focused "Walker S" line. The remainder is supported by diversification into newer B2C segments such as household robots. Also, the firm's maximum manufacturing output has been lifted from 6,000 as of end-2025 to 10,000+ in early April.

The group's FY26 numbers won't reflect all the shipments. This is because revenue recognition timing is dependent on the progress of deployment. But I believe its turnover will still surge meaningfully this year due to the ambitious delivery plan.

Separately, UBTRF has guided at the briefing that its 2026 GPM could widen to the low-40s range. This is achievable based on my analysis.

In the previous section, I drew attention to technological advancements pertaining to product weight and sensors, which should translate into cost savings.

UBTRF's increased size in terms of deliveries/capacity will give it greater supplier clout. I think that will lead to lower-than-anticipated input expenses.

The results meeting commentary highlighted the targeted tripling of "real-world data collection to over 1 million hours in 2026." My view is that this boost to model training could cap future upgrading-related investments.

Also, management sees the proportion of sales contributed by high-margin humanoid offerings rising further from the 44% last year.

You Should Weigh These Downside Factors

The company may report weaker-than-expected gross margins going forward. This could be driven by negative scale economies, assuming deliveries slow.

UBTRF can be affected by industry-wide dynamics even if it performs well. There might be a broad-based sell-off in the humanoid robot sector when investors lose interest in this "hot" theme.

My Closing Remarks

I'm a bull on this name.

The consensus next twelve months' P/S multiples for UBTRF and listed comparable Shenzhen Dobot (2432.HK) are 13x and 19x, respectively. I sourced these valuation metrics from S&P Capital IQ.

Its shares have a potential upside of +46% (19/13), as per my peer benchmarking exercise.

I've already identified re-rating triggers associated with accelerated revenue and narrowing deficits.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: CoreWeave: Slew Of Positive Updates (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
CoreWeave, Inc. (CRWV) is one of several companies at the epicenter of the massive AI infrastructure build. The company quickly gained traction after its IPO as investors wanted to gain direct AI exposure, and CoreWeave had a growing list of impressive customers. Across the AI trade, the infrastructure build-out has been a closely watched battleground.

After initially being bullish on the stock in mid-2025, several execution challenges caused my thesis to turn bearish. While timing was certainly on my side, the stock's ongoing pullback better reflected the risk factors that initially caused my bearish fear. A strong update last quarter left me feeling more opportunistic about the opportunity, and now I believe the stock presents a very attractive investment for long-term holders.

The stock has gone through several periods of heightened volatility, which I believe will persist. As a prominent player in the AI infrastructure build, CRWV will be subject to the wide swings in sentiment as well as any potential concerns around the supply chain and demand environment.

In many ways, CRWV presents one of the best ways to get direct AI exposure in an investment portfolio. The recent challenges around the supply chain seem to be settled, and despite the current geopolitical uncertainties (i.e., wars, potential economic sanctions, etc.), the company's fundamentals remain strong, while valuation is now much more attractive.

The overall momentum around the AI infrastructure build is healthy, with the larger hyperscalers increasing their capital expenditures to support the build. Demand is far outweighing supply, which I believe puts CRWV in a great position to benefit over the next several years.

Recent Positive Updates Are Encouraging

When it comes to the quarterly report, CRWV is a unique asset in that the heavy upfront investments are not aligned with revenue recognition. There is often a lag between when these investments are made and when the computing power at the data centers comes online. This timing difference was on display during the recent Q4 quarter.

Adjusted operating income margin came in at just 6% during the quarter, materially below the 16% recorded in the year-ago period. This was lower than expected due to CRWV deploying infrastructure ahead of internal expectations. This mismatch caused higher expenses during the quarter, with the revenue not yet being recognized. While this is something to monitor, I believe the company made material operational progress over the past few months that far outweighs this dynamic.

Most importantly, the company continues to deliver on its power builds. CRWV now has 850+ MW of active power, compared to 360+ MW at the end of 2024. This includes over 3.1 GW of contracted power, meaning there remains significantly more demand than current capacity.

During 2025, CRWV added 11 data centers, bringing the total active data centers to 43. Investors closely follow these metrics as they are great indicators for success around the build-out. The more data centers that come online, the quicker CRWV can deliver on its contracts, and the more likely they will end up winning more.

This dynamic is clearly seen in revenue backlog, which now stands at $66.8 billion, with only 42% of that expected to be realized over the next 2 years. Thus, CRWV has high visibility into multi-year revenue stream. Much of this revenue backlog growth over the past year is being driven by deals from various AI labs, hyperscaler cloud customers, enterprises, and AI natives.

There is almost a virtuous cycle across the infrastructure build. The more data centers and power CRWV is able to bring online, the more companies want to contract with CRWV. The more committed revenue CRWV has, the more CapEx can be spent on building out the data centers. Rinse and repeat.

For the full year, the company expects revenue of $12-13 billion, adjusted operating income of $900-1,000 million, and CapEx of $30-35 billion. From a power standpoint, the company expects to double active power capacity to >1.7 GW by year-end, with ARR reaching $17-19 billion by 2026 and >$30 billion by 2027. Clearly, there is a lot of momentum.

One big concern around all of this spend is with financing. CRWV had ~$21 billion of debt and ~$3 billion of cash at the end of Q4, which reflects the company's commitment to financing the CapEx.

In late March, CRWV announced the first-ever HPC infrastructure term loan with an investment-grade rating, raising $8.5 billion.

The DDTL 4.0 Facility received ratings of A3 by Moody’s and A (low) by DBRS, respectively, representing the first investment-grade rated financing secured by HPC infrastructure and an associated customer contract.

The structure enables CoreWeave to borrow up to approximately $7.5 billion initially, with the ability to increase total borrowing capacity to $8.5 billion as underlying assets reach stabilization. The facility is designed to provide enhanced access to low-cost capital to support CoreWeave’s continued investment to meet customer demand.

I believe this marks a very important step forward for CRWV. Achieving an investment-grade rating means Moody's does not view the risks as excessive relative to the current debt load. I believe much of this is supported by the company's revenue backlog and continued wins.

Over the past month, CRWV has announced several notable wins, including an agreement to power Perplexity's AI inference workloads, an integration with Cline to power high-performance autonomous engineering, and a deal with Zonos to power its AI-driven solutions.

While none of these deals are the massive multi-billion that will accelerate the curve, they all represent strategic wins that will propel CRWV's competitive positioning.

Valuation

With emerging technologies - such as AI infrastructure - valuation becomes much more of an art than a science. In the case of CRWV, investors have a reference point of >$30 billion in ARR by 2027 to work off of.

The company's visibility into multi-year revenue stream gives me a high degree of confidence that the current valuation is underappreciated.

With a current market cap of ~$47 billion and net debt of ~$18 billion at the end of Q4, enterprise value stands at ~$65 billion. Assuming the company achieves the >$30 billion of ARR target by 2027, this implies a valuation a little over 2x that figure.

Of course, revenue is not the same as ARR, but directionally this points to an undervalued stock.

Risks

One of the biggest risks to my bullish thesis is the "unknown" factor around demand and supply chain. As seen in 2025, disruptions to the supply chain have immediate, direct effects on CRWV's ability to deliver on its data center builds. If the supply chain were to be negatively impaired for a long duration, this could result in CRWV losing contracts or generating greater losses.

Another concern is around the significant amounts of compute intensity required for training and inference. Some argue that this could result in a never-ending need to spend more on chips, which could hurt CRWV's long-term economic model.

Finally, although CRWV recently received an investment-grade rating on one of its debt pieces, a more levered balance sheet poses greater risk. If revenue recognition were to slow, this could cause more pressure on cash flows and the company's ability to fulfill its debt requirements.

Final Thoughts

The AI infrastructure trade still has a long runway, and CRWV is in a great position to benefit from heightened spending activity. The company has demonstrated its ability to navigate an uncertain, complex supply chain while maintaining high growth in its revenue backlog.

The stock has found some footing in recent weeks, and given the underlying momentum seen in the business, additional upside to revenue and ARR could occur this year.

data.title: Tilray Has Strong Synergies, But Sector Remains Weak
data.description:
Tilray (TLRY) reported strong financial performance for Q3 last week with strong revenue growth and higher gross profit. The company has many new synergies on the table, including the release of a new cannabis brand called Portal, as well as new international distribution partnerships. The company’s stock price is up 48% over the last year, although it still has not recovered from its reverse split. The price is down 25% since I last covered the company in January on Seeking Alpha. The company is undervalued with strong forward synergies, but the sector remains weak due to uncertainty. I continue my rating of a Hold.

There are no new updates on rescheduling cannabis in the US. This month, Attorney General Pam Bondi was replaced by Deputy Attorney General Todd Blanche. Rumors on social media speculate that rescheduling will be completed this month. As of April, the US has begun a pilot program to allow the coverage of CBD products under Medicare. The cannabis sector remains volatile with weak investor sentiment and greater market volatility.

Q3-2026 Results

Tilray reported revenue of $206.7 million, an 11% increase YoY and a 5% decrease QoQ. Gross profit was $55 million, a 6% increase YoY and a 4% decrease QoQ. Profit margins were 26.58% versus 28.02% YoY and 26.43% QoQ.

Cannabis revenue was $64.8 million, an increase of 19% YoY. The amount includes $40 million from Canadian adult-usage medical cannabis, an increase of 8% YoY, and $24.1 million from international sales, an increase of 74% YoY. The increase was due to a higher volume of sales. Revenue from Tilray Pharma (international medical cannabis distribution) was $82.9 million, a 35% increase YoY. The increase was due to product mix and an increase in medical device sales. Revenue from the company’s beverage business in the US was $42.6 million, a decrease of 24% YoY. Revenue from the wellness segment was $16.4 million, an increase of 16% YoY.

Net loss was $26.6 million versus a net loss of $789.4 million YoY and a net loss of $44.9 million QoQ. The high net loss for Q3-2025 was due to a one-time noncash impairment. Adjusted net income for Q3-2026 was $2.4 million. The company had $264.8 million in cash and ST investments at the end of the quarter. Total debt is $260.63 million.

Market consensus for Q4-2026 is $350.49 million, an increase YoY and QoQ. The company will report in July.

Synergies

Tilray has many synergies on the table through partnerships and acquisitions. The company will increase its revenue and operating footprint through these strategies. Tilray is positioned to enter US cannabis markets when rescheduling is complete. The company is considering involvement in the new CBD Medicare pilot program in the US.

In February, the company announced a new partnership with Smartway Pharmaceuticals in the UK. Smartway is a national UK distributor with close ties to the UK healthcare system. The company will carry and distribute Tilray’s medical products.

In February, the company also announced a partnership with the Carlsberg Group, a global brewing organization with a presence in 30 countries. Beginning in 2027, Tilray will produce, market, sell, and distribute two of the company’s beer brands across the US.

In March, the company announced a strategic partnership with Alliance Healthcare in Germany. Tilray Pharma (CC Pharma) will increase its access to German pharmacies from 13K to more than 16K. Tilray will be a main supplier to a collective of pharmacy networks throughout Germany.

In March, the company also announced the acquisition of BrewDog, a UK craft beer brand. Tilray will acquire the company’s global brand, its brewing operations in the UK and Ireland, and 11 brewpubs. Tilray expects $200 million per year from the new operations. Tilray acquired BrewDog Australia through a separate transaction. The deal includes brewing and production facilities in Australia, as well as a portfolio of BrewDog pubs.

In April, the company released a new brand of cannabis products called Portal, consisting of infused pre-rolls and vapes. The products boast a high THC potency. The flower is coated in hash diamonds with an average potency of 40%. The vapes will contain liquid hash diamonds with a potency between 95% and 99%.

Although 1-year price performance has improved across the board, the sector remains weak, and prices are on the downtrend. Tilray has not recovered from its reverse split. Canopy Growth is falling below $1 per share and may have to undergo a reverse split. Uncertainty around cannabis rescheduling, as well as greater market volatility, is keeping the prices down. Volatility is high.

Investment Strategy and Risk

Tilray is at low risk of poor financial performance. Its balance sheet is strong. The company has many synergies at play, which will increase revenue and operating footprint. International expansion will benefit the company.

A Hold rating comes at a moderate to high risk. The sector has been volatile, and stock prices are on the downtrend. Rescheduling may cause a small rally in the sector. The current performance of Tilray may not be enough to lift the stock price.

Conclusion

Tilray reported strong financial results last week. The company has strong synergies going into the next year. Tilray has released a new cannabis brand, and it has increased its international operations. Uncertainty around rescheduling has caused the cannabis sector to remain weak. Geopolitical strife is also keeping the market volatile. Although the company is undervalued, I continue my rating of a Hold.

data.title: YETI: A Better Bargain As International Sales Rise (Upgrade)
data.description:
Amid a lopsided and choppy stock market this year, the best thing for investors to be is active market-watchers. Valuations and fundamental stories are changing rapidly, which makes it necessary for us to adjust our positions and monitor more closely.

YETI (YETI), the consumer products company that is best known for its lineup of hard coolers and drinkware, is a company on which I’m growing more positive. Since the start of the year, shares of YETI have fallen ~20%, interrupting a fierce rebound rally that began last year. While I don’t think the stock is necessarily a "Buy" yet, multiples are looking more appealing, especially amid recent improvements in sales.

I last wrote a "Sell" article on YETI in January, when the stock was sitting at $47 per share. Since then, a number of factors have moved in a positive direction for YETI. First and foremost, valuation multiples have collapsed ~25%, and especially when we look forward to FY27 estimates, the stock is trading at a reasonably compelling P/E. Second, recent sales trends have improved, notably in international markets. With all of this factored in, I’m raising YETI back up to a "Neutral" rating. I believe it’s time to add this stock back to our watch lists, though there are still uncertainties on the horizon that need clarity before I can be fully bullish on this name.

To me, at current share prices, YETI is a mixed bag of positives and negatives. On the bright side for this company:

  • YETI’s brand reach is extending into expansive white space, especially overseas. International is a huge opportunity for YETI, with revenue growing at a high teens pace and still a relatively small contribution to the overall company at ~20% of sales.
  • High margins for a consumer products company. YETI makes relatively heavy products in a quite competitive industry. Its high 50s/low 60s gross margin profile signals the substantial brand equity the company has built up and gives the company plenty of headroom to generate economies of scale.

At the same time, however, we have to remain cautious of several risks:

  • Stagnant U.S. sales. The company’s revenue in the United States is pretty flat. This may signal saturation in core markets plus competition from the likes of Stanley.
  • Tariff headwinds. The fate of Trump’s reciprocal tariffs remains a wild card for calendar 2026 and beyond. For the moment, tariffs are a substantial ~2 point headwind to the company’s gross and operating margins.
  • Macro concerns. Weak consumer spending may dent sales, particularly in wholesale channels where resellers may be wary to take on slow-moving inventory.

All in all, I think YETI remains a watch-and-wait stock. The stock’s -20% re-rating over the past few months combined with sales acceleration makes it more compelling, but not a screaming buy just yet. Add this stock to your watchlist, but don’t rush in to buy.

Q4 Recap

Let’s now go through YETI’s latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

YETI’s revenue grew 7% y/y to $583.7 million, beating Wall Street’s expectations of $582.4 million by a slight margin. We note that this is an acceleration versus 2% full-company growth in Q3 and a marked improvement as well relative to a 4% decline in Q2.

Still, some issues persisted. U.S. sales declined 1% y/y, in line with Q3. The company’s sales improvement was entirely from its international arm, where revenue grew 16% y/y and accelerated versus 14% y/y in Q3. We note that we should expect international sales to decelerate in FY26 as the company laps its launch in Japan in Q2 of FY25.

The core risk for investors to track in recent performance is the fact that U.S. retail is becoming very promotional. Brands are slicing prices to entice customers with deals. At the same time, wholesalers are wary of loading up on inventory for fear of slow-moving product. If channel sales are weak, in the long run this could lead to YETI products being less prominently placed on retail shelves.

Per CFO Michael McMullen’s remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

In Drinkware, sales grew 6% to $380 million. As we have noted, 2025 was a challenging year for Drinkware, reflecting U.S. market dynamics and the impact of our supply chain transformation. That said, we consistently communicated our expectations for improvement in Q4, and we were pleased to see that come through our results.

Growth was driven by innovation, strong international demand and continued positive consumer response to our broad assortment in this category. In the U.S., Drinkware sales were flat year-over-year, despite a promotional market and continued cautious wholesale buying.

The issue here is that YETI may feel more competitive pressure to bring pricing down to stimulate sales, especially to wholesale partners. Meanwhile, margins are already on thin ice thanks to tariffs. As shown below, YETI’s gross margin in Q4 fell 180bps y/y to 58.4%. The company notes that it faced a -310bps headwind from tariffs, which was partially offset by the company’s price increases implemented in 2025.

Meanwhile, this was compounded by opex rising 10% y/y, faster than revenue growth. This led to operating margins falling 370bps y/y and operating income dollars falling 14% y/y. EPS also fell 8% y/y to $0.92, though this did still beat Street expectations of $0.88 with 5% upside.

To me, sluggish U.S. sales and a faltering operating margin are the core issues that YETI needs to solve before I can confidently buy into this stock.

Valuation and Key Takeaways

Of course, we acknowledge as well that amid uncertainties, YETI’s valuation has also fallen at a commensurate level. At current share prices near $38, YETI trades at a $2.85 billion market cap. After netting off the $188.3 million in cash against $73.5 million of debt on the company’s current balance sheet, its resulting enterprise value is $2.74 billion.

For FY26, YETI has guided to 6%-8% sales growth, $200-$225 million in FCF, and 12%-14% EPS growth to a midpoint of $2.80.

This positions YETI’s valuation multiples at:

  • 12.8x EV/FY26 FCF
  • 13.5x FY26 P/E

These cheaper multiples are a decent offset against YETI's operating risks. That said, we also think the recent market slide has created plenty of bargain-hunting opportunities, particularly in the tech/software space, so YETI's low multiples aren't exactly unique among small/mid-cap stocks in this market environment.

Add this stock to your watchlist, but remain on the sidelines for now.
